summary: Julia Evans implements traceroute in just 16 lines of Python using the Scapy library. She explains how IP packets have a TTL (Time-To-Live) attribute that decrements at each hop, and how traceroute exploits this by sending packets with incrementally increasing TTLs. When a packet's TTL expires, the intermediate router sends back an ICMP error, revealing its address. The implementation sends UDP packets with increasing TTLs and checks whether the reply indicates a timeout (keep going) or a destination reached (done).
key_points: Scapy lets you construct and send custom network packets in Python with minimal code, making protocol experimentation accessible
Traceroute works by exploiting the TTL field in IP packets — each router decrements it, and when it hits zero, the router sends back an ICMP error revealing its address
A basic traceroute implementation only needs to send UDP packets with incrementing TTLs and check the ICMP reply type
Unix traceroute typically sends UDP packets while Windows traceroute sends ICMP packets; tcptraceroute sends TCP packets
money_quotes: IP packets have a ttl attribute, which stands for "Time-To-Live". Every time a machine receives an IP packet, it decreases the ttl by 1 and passes it on. Basically this is a super smart way to make sure that packets don't get into infinite loops.
If a packet's ttl runs out before it replies, the last machine sends back an ICMP packet saying "sorry, failed!".
I thought it would take a long time, but turns out that (a basic version) is really easy.
To implement traceroute, we send out a UDP packet with ttl=i for i = 1,2,3,... Then we look at the reply packet and see if it's a "Time ran out" or "That port doesn't exist" error message.
So it turns out traceroute is kind of easy!
key_insight: Traceroute is a surprisingly simple concept — just send packets with incrementing TTLs and read the ICMP errors back — and can be implemented in about 15 lines of Python with the right library.
